title: Frontier models write correct Lua against PlexusModule stub
description:
# Story: Frontier models write correct Lua against PlexusModule stub

## Intent

As a Plexus architect, I want quantitative evidence that current frontier LLMs can write correct Plexus-flavored Tactus Lua against a small boot prompt and on-demand documentation, so that I can decide whether to commit several months of work to the runtime-platform direction.

## Gherkin

```gherkin
Feature: Frontier models can author Plexus-flavored Tactus Lua

  Scenario: Single-turn first-try success
    Given a stub PlexusModule exposing read-only operations and a stubbed long-running evaluation
    And a boot prompt of ≤2k tokens taught via the execute_tactus tool description
    And the doc surface plexus.docs.{list,get} is populated with namespace references
    And a representative task from the curated 10–15 task set
    When a frontier LLM (claude-4.6-sonnet, claude-4.7-opus, gpt-5.3-codex, or gemini-3.1-pro) is asked to perform the task using only execute_tactus
    Then the model writes Lua that runs successfully on the first attempt
    And the resulting return value matches the task's expected outcome

  Scenario: Aggregate proceed gate
    Given the spike has been run across the full 10–15 task set against all 4 models
    When the per-model first-try success rates are tallied
    Then at least 2 of the 4 models achieve ≥85% first-try success rate

  Scenario: Failure-mode classification
    Given a task that fails on the first attempt
    When the failure is examined
    Then the failure is classified as one of: API design issue, boot prompt issue, language/Lua issue, or fundamental "models can't do this"
    And each classification informs a specific remediation or escalation
```

## Definition of Done

- All representative tasks executed against all 4 models with full transcripts captured.
- Per-model success rates computed and recorded.
- Aggregate gate evaluated and a go/no-go decision documented.
- Failure-mode classifications produced for every failed attempt.
